definition: Theurgy is 'god-working' — the high, sacramental magic by which the Neoplatonists sought union with the divine, as against the coercive low magic of *goetia*.
history: Greek *theourgia*, 'god-working' (*theos* + *-ergon*) — the high, sacramental magic of the Neoplatonists Iamblichus and Proclus, aimed at the soul's ascent, not at commanding spirits. It became medieval magic's alibi: *we work piously toward God, not with demons*. See the full Word History.

def_sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels
def_sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

commentary: Claire Fanger's work reframed a whole class of medieval ritual magic as *theurgy* rather than demonic conjuring. The *Ars Notoria* and John of Morigny's *Liber Visionum* sought angelic knowledge through prayer and vision — operations their authors understood as devout, not diabolical. Reading them as theurgy (the Neoplatonic 'god-work' of Iamblichus) rather than *goetia* recovers how the practitioners saw themselves, and dissolves the tidy line the Church tried to draw between sanctioned religion and forbidden magic.

commentary_sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices
commentary_sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

commentary_sources.label: Claire Fanger (ed.), Conjuring Spirits
commentary_sources.abstract: A collection of scholarly essays examining actual medieval manuscripts used for ritual magic - instructions for summoning angels or demons, memory-boosting rites, and divination texts - to show how educated Christians in the Middle Ages practiced and argued about magic.

commentary_sources.label: Claire Fanger, Rewriting Magic (John of Morigny)
commentary_sources.abstract: A close study of a 14th-century French monk's own written account of creating a prayer ritual he believed could instantly grant knowledge — a project that got his book publicly condemned and burned as heresy in 1323. Fanger uses his firsthand story to explore how medieval people justified using something so close to forbidden magic.

etymology.note: The Neoplatonists’ high, sacramental magic — working WITH the gods to ascend — as opposed to goēteia, the low magic of coercion.
etymology.hook: High magic vs low magic, in two Greek words.

essay.title: Theurgy: the high magic that worked on the gods
essay.dek: Greek 'god-working' — the sacramental magic of Iamblichus and Proclus, and the alibi that made a thousand years of learned magic respectable.
essay.html:
Theurgy — Greek theourgia, 'god-working' (theos + -ergon) — was the highest magic the ancient world knew: not the commanding of spirits but the sacramental ritual by which the later Neoplatonists, Iamblichus and Proclus, sought to purify the soul and ascend toward union with the divine. The RenaissanceMagicDB glosses it as 'the system of ritual practices… through which the soul achieves communion with the divine,' and sets it at the opposite pole from goetia, the low magic of coercing demons.

What keeps theurgy a live word rather than a museum piece is that this high/low split became the central defense of learned magic for a thousand years. Claire Fanger's Invoking Angels and Sophie Page's Magic in the Cloister show medieval monks adopting the theurgic frame — we are not conjuring demons, we are working piously toward God — to make angel-magic defensible. Michael Bailey's The Sacred and the Sinister maps exactly where the authorities drew, and redrew, that line.

Magic: The Gathering has no theurgy keyword, and the corpus shows it as a rare, literary word — which is the point. The distinction between holy and unholy magic, so central to real history, mostly dissolves in a game where all five colors cast freely. Theurgy is the word for the boundary the game erased.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. theurgia, theurgic, nous, goetia
Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices, 13th–16th Centuries
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

sources.label: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
sources.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.
